What People Do Wrong: Put all business logic validation and database queries directly in the controller
Why Its Bad: Controllers become untestable, unreusable, and hard to maintain. Changes ripple through the entire controller
Better Approach: Extract business logic to Services. Use Form Requests for validation. Use Resources for response formatting
Detection: PHPStan rule for controller line count; manual code review

What People Do Wrong: Concatenate user input directly into SQL strings instead of using parameterized queries or ORM
Why Its Bad: SQL injection vulnerabilities; no protection from malformed input; hard to maintain
Better Approach: Use Eloquent or Query Builder. When raw SQL is needed use bindings
column13: PHPStan; search for DB::select with string interpolation

What People Do Wrong: Trust user input and pass it directly to database or business logic without validation
Why Its Bad: Security vulnerabilities, data corruption, unexpected errors, hard-to-debug issues
Better Approach: Always validate input with Form Requests or $request->validate()

What People Do Wrong: Access Eloquent relationships inside loops triggering a separate query for each iteration
Why Its Bad: Performance degrades linearly with data. 100 posts = 101 queries instead of 2
Better Approach: Use eager loading with with(). Enable preventLazyLoading() in development

What People Do Wrong: Overuse __get __set __call to create dynamic properties losing type safety and IDE support
Why Its Bad: No IDE autocompletion, no static analysis, runtime errors, impossible to refactor, hard to understand code
Better Approach: Use explicit typed properties and methods
Detection: PHPStan strict rules; deprecation of dynamic properties in PHP 8.2

What People Do Wrong: Create a single service class with dozens of methods handling everything in a domain
Why Its Bad: Violates Single Responsibility. Hard to test, maintain, and understand. Merge conflicts in teams
Better Approach: Split into focused Action classes or small services with 1-3 public methods
Detection: Count methods per class; PHPStan max methods rule

What People Do Wrong: Catch \\Exception or \\Throwable without differentiating error types or silently swallowing errors
Why Its Bad: Hides bugs, makes debugging impossible, can leave the system in an inconsistent state
Better Approach: Catch specific exception types. Log unexpected ones. Never swallow silently
Detection: Search for catch (\\Exception with empty body

What People Do Wrong: Hardcode values like URLs database credentials timeouts and feature flags directly in code
Why Its Bad: Changes require code modification and redeployment. Secrets in source control. Environment-specific values wrong
Better Approach: Use .env for secrets. config() for app settings. Database for runtime settings
Detection: Search for hardcoded URLs API keys and magic numbers

What People Do Wrong: Make multiple database changes without wrapping them in a transaction
Why Its Bad: Partial updates on failure leave data in inconsistent state. Half-created orders, orphaned records
Better Approach: Wrap related database operations in DB::transaction()
Detection: Search for multiple create/update calls without DB::transaction

What People Do Wrong: Put business logic validation or database queries directly in route definitions
Why Its Bad: Route files become unmanageable. Logic scattered across routes controllers and services
Better Approach: Routes should only define URL to controller mapping. Use middleware for cross-cutting logic
Detection: Check routes directory for closures with business logic

What People Do Wrong: Use Facades everywhere hiding true class dependencies and making testing harder
Why Its Bad: Invisible dependency graph. Tests need Facade mocking. Hard to tell what a class actually needs
Better Approach: Use constructor injection. Reserve Facades for Blade templates and simple scripts
Detection: Count Facade usage in service classes; PHPStan

What People Do Wrong: Leave $guarded empty or use $request->all() with unprotected models
Why Its Bad: Users can modify any database column by adding fields to the request
Better Approach: Define $fillable whitelist on every model. Use $request->validated() not $request->all()

What People Do Wrong: Eager load entire relationships without constraints fetching far more data than needed
Why Its Bad: Loads unnecessary data consuming memory and bandwidth. Joins all columns of related models
Better Approach: Use constrained eager loading to load only what you need
Detection: Laravel Debugbar query count; check memory usage

What People Do Wrong: Reference routes and controller methods as strings which break silently on rename
Why Its Bad: Route references break silently. No IDE navigation. Renaming a method won't update references
Better Approach: Use route names or controller::class syntax

What People Do Wrong: Rely only on log files for error tracking in production
Why Its Bad: Errors go unnoticed. No alerting. No error grouping or trends. Hard to reproduce issues
Better Approach: Use Sentry Bugsnag or similar for real-time error tracking with context

What People Do Wrong: Store passwords as plaintext or use weak hashing like MD5/SHA1
Why Its Bad: Database breach exposes all passwords. MD5/SHA1 can be reversed with rainbow tables
Better Approach: Always use bcrypt or Argon2id via Hash::make()
Detection: Search for md5( sha1( in password context

What People Do Wrong: Leave APP_DEBUG=true in production exposing detailed error info including credentials
Why Its Bad: Set APP_DEBUG=false in production. Use custom error pages. Use Sentry for error tracking
Code Good: Critical
Severity: Check .env on production; automated deployment checks

What People Do Wrong: Make HTTP calls to external APIs synchronously in the request cycle making users wait
Why Its Bad: User waits for slow external API responses. Timeouts cascade. App performance depends on third parties
Better Approach: Queue external calls. Use async HTTP. Cache responses
Detection: Check controller methods for Http:: and Mail:: calls

What People Do Wrong: Return Eloquent models directly from API endpoints exposing all database columns
Why Its Bad: Use API Resources (JsonResource) to control exactly what fields are exposed
Code Good: High

What People Do Wrong: Two or more classes depend on each other creating tight coupling that is impossible to test or modify
Why Its Bad: Service A depends on Service B which depends on Service A
Better Approach: Extract shared logic into a third class. Use events or interfaces to break the cycle
Detection: PHPStan circular dependency detection; container error on boot
